title: 第 42 節
en: In what sense could real rivers like the Ganga and Yamuna be said to be hemmed in or kept captive by Ahi, if they had not been connected by the poets with water poured down from the clouds ? The very language, here applied to the rivers of rain, occurs in passages which clearly refer to real rivers, fed and kept alive by rain. Rivers in India often run dry, and disappear altogether, but they reappear after the season of thunderstorms, the Varsha, or the rainy season, and all this was looked upon as the work of Indra. Hence we find that the rain waters are called sindhu, or streams where there can be no doubt that they are meant for rain, for it is said the sun had drawn them up to the clouds ; for instance, Rv. VII, 47, 4 : Y&h su'rya/i rasmibhiA atatana, yahhy&h indra/?, aradat gatiim urmim, Te, sindhavaA (voc), ' The waters which the sun attracted and for which Indra made a path, those streams, te sindhavaA,' &c.

Even in a hymn such as III, 33, 6, which is clearly addressed to real rivers, the rivers are introduced as saying : ' Indra holding the thunderbolt has dug us out, he struck down VWtra, who encompassed the rivers, the good-handed SavitW (sun) led us ; at his command we, the streams, are moving on.'
And again in the next verse it is said that Indra killed those who surrounded (the water), and that the waters (apas), (here clearly meant for the rivers) wishing for a path, moved along.

If then even with us the rivers which are dry in summer are swollen and flooded in the autumn, so that they cover miles of land as with a deluge, how much more was this the case with the mountainstreams of the Panjab. Bergaigne (ii, p. 184) was Vl] INDRA IN THE VEDA. 755 perfectly right that in the actual fight between Indra and VWtra, Indra was looked upon by the ifo'shis as the conqueror of VWtra, not as the direct giver or producer of rain, like Par^/anya. For the same reason it is quite intelligible also that the verb vrish, to pour down rain, should not be used of Indra. But Indra remained for all that, abgit, the conqueror of the water, it was he who broke open the stable of the cows, it was he who set the captive rivers free (IV, 19, 5). He was in fact, though indirectly, what his name declared, Indra, the moistener of the land.

I have tried to prove in another place that Soma, so intimately connected with Indra, was in one of its acceptations, if not in its original sense, the rain (su = v-ei) ; and that there are passages in the Veda where Soma is not only compared, but is actually identified with rain, has been shown by Professor Oldenberg, p. 459, note. Thus we read, I, 32, 12 : Ag&y&h g&h dgaysJi sura somam, ' Thou, hero, hast conquered the cows, thou hast conquered the Soma,' which, in this and similar passages, I should venture to translate simply by rain. The preparation of the Soma-juice at the Soma-sacrifice seems often a conscious imitation of the process by which the rain was supposed to be poured down through the clouds ; nay, Soma is actually called Vrotrahan, and implored to send rain from heaven 1.

But though Indra under this name was a more recent and peculiarly Indian god, the possibility is 1 This has been fully worked out by Hillebrandt, in his Vedische Mythologie, 1891 ; see also Oldenberg, Eeligion des Veda, p. 459 ; Bergaigne, Eeligion Vedique, i, p. 214 seq.
756 INDRA IN THE VEDA. [chap.

by no means excluded that some of the myths told of Indra were of earlier date, that they were told originally of some other god manifested in the sky and the storm, such as Dyaus, or Par^anya, and that they gathered afterwards round this new name. We have only to accept Indra in the Veda as the conqueror of water (abgit), and everything else that is told of him can easily be accounted for as springing from this original character.
Andra.

If, as is now supposed, Andra in the Avesta was not meant for Indra, we should have to look upon Indra as later even than the separation of the Aryas of India and Persia, but the occurrence of Verethraghna as the genius of victory makes us hesitate. Verethraghna presupposes VWtrahan, a constant epithet of Indra in the Veda. Indra's chief duty was, as we saw, to kill Vro'tra, i. e. the coverer, a demon who keeps the rain imprisoned in the cloud, and at the same time keeps the light of the day captive. Hence VHtra in later Sanskrit came to be a name for cloud, as well as for enemy in general. It is important to remark that though Vritra, as a demon, is not mentioned in the Avesta, yet verethraghna, meaning literally, VWtrakilling, could not have assumed the meaning which it has in the Avesta, viz. victorious, except for the antecedents which we can see in the Veda. But for the victory of Indra over VWtra, darkness or clouds, as celebrated in the Veda, vritraghna could never have assumed the meaning of victorious, so that we must admit a knowledge of the demon Vritra, and of his destroyer, though not yet under Vl] ANDRA. 757 the name of Indra, in the background of Avestic mythology also. We must remember that in the Veda it is not only Indra, but Agni also, Soma, the Asvins and the host of the Maruts who are called vWtrahan, or Vritra-killing, demon -killing. This is all the more important, because it justifies the occurrence of Vritra as "Opdpos in Greek mythology, and of Azhi = Ahi in the Avesta.
Indra, an Agent.

It should be remembered that the name of Indra expresses by its very form an agent, and not a visible object of nature. We saw before that all mythological names expressed originally agents, that even Dyaus was a name of the sky as an active power, before it was used as a name of the objective sky, that Ushas was not simply the red light of the morning, but the agent who brought the light, and was active in that light, nay that even Agni, before it became the recognised name of the material fire, meant the swift mover. Still, there is this difference that Indra, being from the first conceived as the causer of rain, was never identified with any physical object, whether rain or cloud, or light or sky, but at once grew into a dramatic character, a hero, a conqueror, a supreme ruler.
Indra Supreme.

In that capacity Indra came naturally into competition with other gods, such as Dyaus and Varuwa, and even Agni. Though called the son of Dyaus, Indra is actually praised for having excelled, and in the end replaced his father l.
1 Science of Language, vol. ii, p. 543.
758 INDRA SUPREME. [chap.

As to Vanma, he held his own in the thoughts of his worshippers, but some rivalry between the two must have been recognised, as there is actually in the Eig-veda a dialogue between Indra and Vanma, in which each asserts his supremacy, Vanma trusting more, as it would seem, in his long-established character, Indra in the works which he had performed. This change in the supremacy is clearly alluded to in a hymn of the tenth Ma?2<iala, 1 24, 4, ' Many years I sacrificed here ; choosing Indra, I leave the father (Dyaus ?). Agni, Soma, Vanma, these go ; the kingship turns, I approach and cherish this ' (i.e. Indra). Agni also was praised for performing the very acts in which Indra displayed his prowess, and it would be easy to collect passages in which every one of these gods, Dyaus, Vanma, Agni, and Indra, was praised as supreme. Here we see once more this peculiar stage of thought, which seems to us almost inconceivable, but which is of the greatest importance for a proper understanding of the development of the religion of India, and for which I suggested the name of Henotheism, not as if it were a necessary phase in the growth of all religious thought, which few would admit even in the case of Fetishism or Totemism, but simply as a convenient comprehensive title for actual facts which are familiar to every Vedic scholar.
Vritra, Ahi.

The enemy who is slain by Indra, whether he is called Vn'tra or Ahi (serpent), was probably as old as, if not older, than Indra. VWtra is called, I, 32, 4, prathamaepas ahinam, the firstborn of serpents, and his mother also is called Ahi and Danu, Rv. I, Vl] ViZ/TRA, AHI. 759 32, 9. Hence though Indra's name is absent from other Aryan mythologies, we can quite understand that that of Vn'tra should appear as Orthros. Ahi, also the serpent, originally the throttler, the enemy of the bright gods and of all that is bright in nature, occurs under various forms in other Aryan mythologies. In the Avesta he is mentioned as Azhi, particularly as Azhi dahaka (Zohak). In Greek Echis and Ophis would both correspond to Ahi, and we find Echi-dna as the name of a monster, the daughter of Tartaros and Ge (or of Chrysaor and Kallirrhoe), the wife of Typhon, and the mother of Chimaira, of Orthros, the dragon in Kolchis, of the Sphinx, Kerberos, Skylla, Gorgon, and other horrible creatures. She was killed by Argos Panoptes, while Typhon, Typhaon, or Typhoeus was slain by Zeus K There is another derivative, Echion, the name of one of the Gigantes who fought against Zeus. Ophis, serpent, has not assumed mythological independence in Greece, but Ophion is again the name of one of the Titans and one of the Gigantes. The same being appears in Greece under such names as Drakon, dragon, or Python, the son of Gaia, whom Apollon killed at Delphoi. The name of Pyth-on seems connected with ttvOixtjv, bottom, Sk. budhna for budhma. The root of TTvdaiv was *bhudh, in Greek ttv9, and this with the suffix cov gave irvdoiv. This name has nothing to do with ttvOoj, to rot, but with TTvO-fxrjv, 1 There is some confusion between these names, Typhaon being sometimes represented as the son of Typhoeus. The general idea, however, which they expressed in the eyes of the Greeks, was the same, that of violent storms darkening the sky, also of volcanoes vomiting fire.
760 VEITKA, AHI. [chap.

the lowest bottom, or, as we should say, the bottomless pit, possibly the abyss of the sea, from which in the Yeda another demon, Ahir budhnyaA, derived his name, a kind of vE^t? ttvOcovlo^. The fates of Ahi in Teutonic mythology have been traced by Mannhardt in such demons as Agi and Oegir, nay in Uoki, in Ecke, and in the faithful Eckart of mediaeval epic poetry \ All these evil powers were powers of darkness, whether of the night or of the black storm-clouds, and Indra, their born and sworn enemy, developed thus naturally the character of a fighting and victorious god.
Dasas.

Nothing was more intelligible, therefore, than that the people of the Vedic age in their conflict with the black inhabitants of the country should have invoked Indra's help. That these enemies or Dasas were originally meant for the black native races of the country whom the Aryas had to subdue, I tried to prove in my Letter to Bunsen in 1856 2. And as the life of the gods is always the reflection of the life of man, it was but natural that the enemies of the gods also should be represented as Dasas. As the Aryas in their conflict with the real Dasas were chiefly fighting for cows and such like booty, being goshu-yudh, fighting among or for cows, the gods also were naturally represented in the same way, as fighting for cows, or as trying to rescue stolen cattle. It might also have happened that some of the women 1 Mannhardt, Germ. My then, p. 93.
2 They are called muradeva, apavrata, anindra, anW/c, slsnadeva, anas, kravyad, amanusha, tvaJc kr/shwa, &c. On modern names of aboriginal inhabitants, see Kittel, Sacrifice, p. 1 6.

Vl] DASAS. 761 of the Aryas had been carried off, and had actually become the wives of the Dasas (dasapatni) ; and as these were rescued by the Aryas, Indra also was invoked to rescue the waters that were kept captive, ahi-gopa/i, guarded by Ahi, as dasapatni/i, or wives of the Dasa \ We must try to enter into this narrow circle of the thoughts of the Vedic Aryas if we want to understand their poetry. When they saw the dark night suddenly opened by the light, their nearest thought was their own dark stable, often a mere cave, which was shut during the night and opened again in the morning. If that conception had once been transferred to the dark night as illumined in the morning, what stepped out of this stable, the bright beams of the morning, could only be to them the cows, the red cows. It was thus that the dawn became to them naturally the red cow, or the mother of the cows. There were thus three kinds of cows, the real cows, the cows in the dark cloud (rain = milk), and the cows stepping forth from the dark stable of the night (the rays of the morning). These three are not always easy to distinguish in the Veda, nay, while we naturally try to distinguish between them, the poets themselves seem to delight in mixing them up. In the passage quoted above we saw how the captive waters were compared to cows that had been stolen by Pam, but what is once compared in the Veda is soon identified. As to the Dawn, she is not only compared to a cow, she is called the cow straight out '-. Tims when we Rv. I, 32, 11 : Dasapatni/j ahigopa/j atish^an nfruddliaA apa7i pam'na iva gava/*, Apam bilam apihitam yat asit vn'tram ^aghanvan a pa tat vavara.
2 Science of Language, vol. ii, p. 583.
■762 DASAS. [chap.

read, Rv. I, 97, 2, ' These dawns have made a light, on the eastern half of the sky, they brighten their splendour, the bright cows approach, the mothers,' the cows, gava/i, can only be the dawns themselves, the plural of dawn being constantly in the Veda used where we should use the singular. In Rv. I, 93, 4, we read that 'Agnishomau deprived Pani of his cows and found light for many.' Here again the cows are the dawns kept by Pam in the dark stable or cave of the night, discovered by Sarama, and delivered every morning by the gods of light.
We read in Rv. I, 62, 3, that BWhaspati split the rock and found the cows.

Of Indra it is said, II, 19, 3, that he produced the sun and found the cows ; of BWhaspati, II, 24, 3, that he drove out the cows, that he split the cave by his word, that he hid the darkness, and lighted up the sky. What can be clearer ? The Maruts also, II, 34, 1, are said to uncover the cows, and Agni, V, 14, 4, is praised for killing the fiends, for having overcome darkness by light, and having found the cows, water, and the sun.
zh: 在什麼意義上，真實的河流如恆河（Ganga）和亞穆納河（Yamuna）可被說成是為阿黑（Ahi）所圍困或所囚禁，倘若詩人們未曾將這些河流與從雲中傾瀉而下的雨水相聯繫？這裡應用於雨水河流的語言本身，也出現在那些明確指涉真實河流的段落中，這些河流由雨水滋養並保持活力。印度的河流常常乾涸，甚至完全消失，但在雷暴季節（Varsha）或雨季之後它們便會重現，而所有這一切都被視為因陀羅（Indra）的作為。因此我們發現雨水被稱為「辛杜」（sindhu），或溪流，在這些情況下毫無疑問它們是指雨水，因為據說太陽已將它們吸引到雲中；舉例來說，《梨俱吠陀》七卷47頌4句：Yáh su'rya/i rasmibhiA atatana, yahhy&h indra/?, aradat gatiim urmim, Te, sindhavaA（呼格）—「太陽以其光線吸引而因陀羅為其開闢道路的那些水流，那些溪流，辛杜河啊」（te sindhavaA），等等。
即使在如《梨俱吠陀》第三卷第 33 讚第 6 頌這樣明確是對真實河流的讚美詩中，這些河流也被介紹為說道：「執持雷電的因陀羅挖掘了我們，他擊倒了圍困河流的毘栗多（Vṛtra），善手的薩維圖爾（Savitṛ，太陽）引領了我們；在他的命令下，我們這些溪流正在流動。」
而在下一節詩句中，又說因陀羅殺死了那些圍繞著水域的人，且那些水（apas）——在此明確指涉河流——渴望尋得一條通路，於是流動而去。

若然即便在我們這裡，夏季乾涸的河流在秋季也會漲水氾濫，覆蓋數英里的土地如同一場洪水，那麼巴夏卜山區溪流的情況更是如此。貝爾蓋涅（Bergaigne）（第二卷，第184頁）的看法完全正確，即在因陀羅（Indra）與弗棱多（Vrtra）的實際戰爭中，因陀羅被瑞師們視為弗棱多的征服者，而非如帕爾詹亞（Parjanya）那樣直接賦予或產生雨水者。基於同樣的理由，動詞 vrish（傾瀉雨水）不被用於因陀羅也完全可以理解。儘管如此，因陀羅仍然保有征服水之物的身分，正是他打破了牲畜的圈舍，正是他釋放了被囚禁的河流（《梨俱吠陀》第四卷，第19首，第5節）。事實上，雖然是間接的，他就是其名字所宣稱的那樣，因陀羅，大地的潤澤者。

我已在其他地方試圖證明，與因陀羅（Indra）密切相關的蘇摩（Soma），在其多重涵義之中（如果不是其本義的話），乃指雨水（su = v-ei）；而且奧登伯格教授已經表明，在吠陀中確實存在若干段落，其中蘇摩不僅被比作，而且被實際上等同於雨水，見第459頁註腳。因此我們讀到《梨俱吠陀》I, 32, 12：Ag&y&h g&h dgaysJi sura somam，「你啊英雄，已征服牛群，你已征服蘇摩」，在此段及類似段落中，我願意大膽地簡單地將其譯為雨水。在蘇摩祭祀中進行蘇摩汁液的製備，似乎經常是對雨水據信被傾倒過雲層過程的有意模仿；不僅如此，蘇摩實際上被稱為破障者（Vrotrahan），並被懇求從天堂降雨。

但雖然因陀羅以此名稱而言是一位較晚期且特別屬於印度的神祇，其可能性 ¹ 希勒布蘭特（Hillebrandt）在其《吠陀神話學》（Vedische Mythologie, 1891）中已充分論證此點；另參見歐登貝格（Oldenberg）的《吠陀宗教》（Religion des Veda, p. 459）與貝爾蓋涅（Bergaigne）的《吠陀宗教》（Religion Vedique, i, p. 214 及以下）。
756 吠陀中的因陀羅。[章]
但並不能排除這樣的可能性，即某些關於因陀羅的神話可能源自更早的時代，它們原本是關於某位其他的提婆而講述的，該提婆顯現於天空和風暴中，例如特尤斯或帕瑞尼亞，爾後這些神話才聚合於這個新的名號周圍。我們只需承認吠陀中的因陀羅乃是水的征服者（abgit），那麼關於他的一切其他記載都可以輕易地歸因於他這一原初的特質。
安德拉。

如果如今所推測的，《阿維斯陀》中的安德拉（Andra）並非為指因陀羅，那麼我們就必須認為因陀羅出現的時間甚至晚於雅利安人的印度與波斯分化之後。然而維瑞特拉格納（Verethraghna）作為勝利的靈神這一事實使我們不得不猶豫。維瑞特拉格納預設了維瑞特拉罕（VWtrahan），這是《吠陀》中因陀羅的常見讚詞。如我們所見，因陀羅的主要職責是殺死毘栗多羅（Vro'tra），即「蔽者」——一個將雨水囚禁於雲中、同時將白晝之光困禁於暗中的惡魔。因此，在後期梵語中，毘栗多（VWtra）既成為「雲」的名稱，亦成為「敵手」的泛稱。值得注意的是，雖然作為惡魔的毘栗多在《阿維斯陀》中並未被提及，然而維瑞特拉格納字面上的意義為「毘栗多-殺滅」（VWtra-killing），它在《阿維斯陀》中之所以能承擔「勝利的」這一含義，實乃由於我們在《吠陀》中所能看到的先行要素。倘非《吠陀》中所讚頌的因陀羅戰勝毘栗多、黑暗或雲靄的勝利，維瑞特拉格納決不可能承擔勝利之意。因此，我們必須承認，在阿維斯陀神話學的背景中，雖未以「因陀羅」之名，但對惡魔毘栗多及其摧毀者的認識是存在的。我們必須記住，在《吠陀》中，不僅因陀羅，阿格尼（Agni）、蘇摩（Soma）、阿濕呾毘（Asvins）及眾多馬魯特（Maruts）也都被稱為維瑞特拉罕（vWtrahan）或毘栗多-殺滅者、惡魔-殺滅者。這一點尤其重要，因為它為希臘神話中維瑞特拉（Ophιος）的出現，以及《阿維斯陀》中的阿日（Azhi = Ahi）的出現提供了正當性。
因陀羅——一位代理者

應當記住，因陀羅（Indra）這個名字在其形式上表達的是一個施動者，而非自然界的可見對象。我們之前已看到，所有神話名稱最初都表達施動者；甚至特尤斯（Dyaus）在被用作客觀天空的名稱之前，乃是作為活動力量的天空之名；烏莎絲（Ushas）並非僅僅是晨曦的紅光，而是帶來光線的施動者，在那光線中活躍著；乃至阿格尼（Agni）在成為公認的物質火之名之前，意指迅疾的運動者。然而，這裡存在著一個差異：因陀羅自始即被設想為降雨的原因者，從未被等同於任何物質對象，無論是雨、雲、光或天空，而是立即成長為一個戲劇性的人物、一位英雄、一位征服者、一位至高無上的統治者。
因陀羅至尊
在此身分中，因陀羅自然而然地與其他提婆產生競爭，例如特尤斯與伐樓拿（Varuwa），甚至阿耆尼（Agni）。雖然因陀羅被稱為特尤斯之子，但他實際上因超越了自己的父親而受到讚頌，並最終取代了他¹。
語言科學，第二卷，第543頁。
758 因陀羅至高無上。[章

關於梵摩（Vanma），他在其信仰者的思想中保持了自己的地位，但兩者之間必然存在某種競爭，因為在梨俱吠陀中實際上有一段因陀羅與梵摩之間的對話，其中各自聲稱自己的至高性。梵摩似乎更信賴其久已確立的性質，而因陀羅則信賴於他所成就的功業。這種至高性的轉變在第十曼陀羅的一首讚歌中明確提及，1 24, 4：「我在此獻祭多年；選擇因陀羅，我舍棄父親（特尤斯？）。阿格尼、蘇摩、梵摩，這些神祇去矣；王權轉移，我親近並推崇此者」（即因陀羅）。阿格尼也因表現出因陀羅展示其威力的同樣行為而受到讚頌，人們很容易蒐集這些段落，其中每一位神祇——特尤斯、梵摩、阿格尼和因陀羅——都被讚頌為至高者。在此我們再度看到這一獨特的思想階段，這對我們來說幾乎難以理解，但對於恰當理解印度宗教的發展卻具有最大的重要性。為此，我建議採用**單一神教（henotheism）**這一名稱，並非暗示它必然是所有宗教思想發展中的一個階段——即使在拜物教或圖騰制的情況下，也鮮有人會承認這一點——而只是作為一個方便的綜合性標題，用來涵蓋吠陀學者熟悉的實際現象。
弗栗多羅（Vritra）、阿喜（Ahi）。

被因陀羅（Indra）所斬殺的敵人，無論稱為弗栗多（Vn'tra）或阿詰（Ahi，蛇），其年代可能與因陀羅同樣古老，甚至更為久遠。弗栗多在《梨俱吠陀》第一卷第三十二章第四節被稱為「蛇之首生者」（prathamaepas ahinam），其母親亦被稱為阿詰與達奴（Danu），見《梨俱吠陀》第一卷第三十二章第九節。因此，雖然因陀羅之名在其他雅利安神話中付諸闕如，我們完全可以理解弗栗多之名何以在希臘神話中出現為俄爾特洛斯（Orthros）。阿詰，亦即蛇，原本是掐死者、光明諸神與自然界一切光明之物的敵人，在其他雅利安神話中以各種形式出現。在《阿維斯陀》中，它被提及為阿茲希（Azhi），尤其是阿茲希‧達哈卡（Azhi dahaka，佐哈克）。在希臘語中，埃基斯（Echis）與俄皮斯（Ophis）皆與阿詰相應，而我們發現埃喀德娜（Echi-dna）乃一怪物之名，為塔爾塔羅斯與蓋亞（或為克律薩俄耳與卡利羅厄）之女，堤豐（Typhon）之妻，以及喀邁拉（Chimaira）、俄爾特洛斯、科爾喀斯之龍、司芬克斯、刻耳柏洛斯（Kerberos）、斯庫拉（Skylla）、戈耳貢與其他可怖生物之母。她被百眼巨人阿爾戈斯（Argos Panoptes）所殺，而堤豐、堤法翁（Typhaon）或堤福耶烏斯（Typhoeus）則被宙斯所斬。另有一衍生名稱埃基翁（Echion），乃與宙斯交戰的巨靈之一。俄皮斯（蛇）在希臘並未獲得神話上的獨立地位，但俄皮翁（Ophion）再次成為泰坦之一與巨靈之一的名字。同一存在在希臘以龍王（Drakon）或皮同（Python）等名稱出現，皮同乃蓋亞之子，為阿波羅在德爾斐所殺者。皮同（Pyth-on）之名似與πύθιον（底部）相連，對應梵文之布達那（budhna，或布達馬 budhma）。πύθιον之根為 *bhudh，在希臘語為 πύθ，此字加上後綴 ον 遂成 πύθοιν。此名與 πύθος（腐爛）無涉，而與 πύθ-μην相關。¹希臘人眼中，這些名稱之間雖有若干混淆（堤豐有時被說成堤福耶烏斯之子），其所表達的總體觀念卻是相同的：暴烈風暴使天黑暗，以及火山噴發烈火。 ¹此處名稱間存有某些混淆，有時堤法翁被視為堤福耶烏斯之子。
760 韋陀、阿狄。[章]

最深的深淵，或如吾人所言，無底的坑穴，可能是大海的深淵，吠陀中另一個惡魔阿希爾·布德尼亞（Ahir budhnyaA）即源於此，一種 ὲς τὰ κάτωθεν αἰῶνα 的東西。特尤斯在日耳曼神話中的命運已被曼哈特追溯至如阿吉（Agi）和厄吉爾（Oegir）等惡魔，乃至烏基（Uoki）、艾克（Ecke），以及中世紀史詩詩歌中忠誠的艾克哈特（Eckart）；所有這些邪惡勢力都是黑暗的力量，無論是夜晚之黑暗或烏雲密佈的風暴，因此因陀羅作為它們天生的死敵，自然發展出戰鬥和勝利之神的性格。
達薩人（Dasas）。

因此，吠陀時代的人民在與該地區黑膚原住民的衝突中援引因陀羅（Indra）幫助，這再也沒有什麼更容易理解的了。這些敵人或達薩人（Dasas）最初指的是雅利安人必須征服的該地區黑膚原住民族，我曾在1856年寄給邦森（Bunsen）的信中試圖證明這一點²。既然眾神的生活始終是人類生活的反映，那麼眾神的敵人也自然而然地被表現為達薩人。由於雅利安人在與真實的達薩人的衝突中主要是為了爭奪牛隻及類似的戰利品而戰鬥，被稱為「護牛之戰」（goshu-yudh），即在牛隻之間或為了牛隻而戰，眾神自然也被同樣地表現為為牛隻而戰，或試圖救回被盜的牲畜。同樣也可能發生的是，有些婦女¹ ¹ Mannhardt，德文版《神話學》，第93頁。
他們被稱為 muradeva、apavrata、anindra、anīkavāc、slsnadeva、anas、kravyad、amanusha、tvāc kr/shwa 等。關於原住民現代名稱，參見 Kittel, *Sacrifice*, p. 16。

梨俱吠陀的雅利安人婦女曾被達薩人劫掠，並實際上成為達薩人的妻子（dasapatni）；當雅利安人救回這些婦女時，因陀羅也被祈求去拯救被囚禁的水流，即阿希所守護的水流（ahi-gopali），視為達薩人的妻子（dasapatni），或達薩的妻子。我們必須試著進入吠陀雅利安人思想的狹隘圈圈，才能理解他們的詩歌。當他們看到黑暗的夜晚突然被光線照亮時，他們最直接的想法是他們自己的黑暗馬廄，常常只是一個山洞，在夜間關閉，早晨再度開啟。倘若這個觀念曾被轉移到被早晨光線照亮的黑暗夜晚，那麼從這個馬廄步出的東西——早晨的光亮光線——對他們而言只能是母牛，紅色的母牛。就這樣，曙光對他們自然而然地成為紅色的母牛，或母牛之母。因此存在三種母牛：真實的母牛、黑暗雲層中的母牛（雨 = 奶）、以及從黑暗夜晚的馬廄中踏出的母牛（早晨的光線）。這三種在吠陀中並不總是容易區分的，更有甚者，儘管我們自然而然地試圖區分它們，詩人們自己似乎樂於將它們混淆在一起。在上面引述的段落中，我們看到被囚禁的水流被比作被帕尼盜走的母牛，但凡在吠陀中曾被比較過的東西，很快便被等同化。至於曙光，她不僅被比作一頭母牛，她被直截了當地稱為母牛。因此當我們看到《梨俱吠陀》I, 32, 11：「達薩之妻，為阿希所守護，被緊緊關閉的水流，如同被盜的母牛，被帕尼奪走。被覆蓋的水流之洞穴，那是真理，毗利特拉被打碎後，他開啟了它」時——
2 《語言科學》，第二卷，第五八三頁。

讀《梨俱吠陀》第一卷97頌第2節：「這些曙光製造了光，在天空的東方，它們照亮了自己的光輝，明亮的牝牛靠近，眾母親，」其中牝牛 *gava/i* 只能是曙光本身，在《吠陀》中曙光的複數形式經常被用於我們應該使用單數的地方。在《梨俱吠陀》第一卷93頌第4節，我們讀到「阿耆尼與蘇摩奪取了帕尼的牝牛，為許多人尋得光明。」這裡牝牛再次是被帕尼關在黑暗的馬廄或夜晚的洞穴中的曙光，由莎羅摩發現，由光明之神每個早晨釋放出來。
我們在《梨俱吠陀》第一卷第62讚第3頌（Rv. I, 62, 3）中讀到，婆哈斯帕蒂（Brhaspati）劈開巨岩，發現了群牛。

據云，因陀羅（Indra）在《梨俱吠陀》第二卷19首3節中產生了太陽並尋得了牛群；婆羅訶斯帕提（Brihaspati）在第二卷24首3節中驅趕出了牛群，以其言語裂開了洞穴，隱沒了黑暗，並照亮了蒼穹。還有什麼比這更清楚的呢？馬魯特神眾（the Maruts）在第二卷34首1節中也被說為揭露了牛群；阿格尼（Agni）在第五卷14首4節中則因殺死了妖魔、以光明戰勝黑暗、以及尋得了牛群、水和太陽而受到讚頌。
